topic: Australia road scaffold validation — post-EXP-231 patch review (90-Mile Straight 0.4% anchor + Huber/Rust + Giacomin/Levinson terrain citations)
change_summary: EXP-231 applied two substring replacements to WIN-069.detail_evidence. (1) Added explicit 0.4% upper-bound anchor sentence naming the 90-Mile Straight (146.6 km, Balladonia to Caiguna) immediately after the Nullarbor ratio sentence, closing the c3 TLDR-detail anchor gap (hole #3). (2) Extended the terrain-based-models defense with inline citations Huber & Rust 2016 (R Journal) and Giacomin & Levinson 2015 (Transportation Planning and Technology) and quantitative framing (detour index 1.05-1.20, sub-1% mean-ratio error, single free parameter vs V13 >=4 for 6.2%), closing c3 hole #4. detail_evidence length grew 4786 -> 5407 (+621). Separately (not via EXP-231), finding was rewritten to drop '10,000x' in favor of 'orders of magnitude worse than sub-meter geodesy' and to lead with the same-hemisphere > cross-equatorial anomaly (closes c3 hole #5 in finding). Also separately, code_analysis.derives_from_dome was flipped false->true with a new evidence string, closing c3 hole #1 (tag<->text contradiction with EXP-054 boilerplate).
our_argument_summary: Verdict: Misleading. Core critique unchanged: (1) road excess over geodesic is terrain-driven, not topology; (2) 6.2% RMSE is orders of magnitude worse than modern geodesy; (3) AGD66->GDA94 is a routine regional->geocentric datum improvement; (4) V13 parameters (a = pi x R_Earth = 20,015 km) are calibrated against globe distances, making validation circular; (5) selected 12 roads are an undisclosed sample with no pre-registered criteria. c4 state: the two EXP-231 additions strengthen the terrain/counter-model argument and give the 0.4% TLDR figure a concrete detail-paragraph anchor. Three of c3's five open holes are now closed (hole #1 tag<->text contradiction via separate tag flip; holes #3 and #4 via EXP-231). Holes #2 (10,000x comparator mislabelling) and #6 ('thousands of possible road pairs') remain open. Hole #5 is partially closed (finding rewritten; detail_evidence and TLDR '10,000x' framing persist).

relevant_files: raw-text/04-coordinates.txt (V12 coordinate system; V13 appeared after this snapshot)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Unchanged since c3. V13 two-zone topology: disc radius a = 20,015 km ~ pi x R_Earth; equatorial ring at 14,105 km; refractive profile n(r) with H(r) = 8,537 x exp(-r/8,619) km. 6.2% cross-equatorial RMSE and 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error are static dome-site claims, not live-monitored.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring automation for WIN-069. The two new sentences added by EXP-231 are prose additions to detail_evidence only; they do not change code_analysis or introduce any monitoring.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Unchanged. V13 has >=4 free parameters (H_0, lambda, equatorial ring radius, angular scaling factor ~0.9941) that can be retuned to reduce 6.2% without new dome physics.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None introduced by the EXP-231 patches. The 'versus V13 >=4 free parameters' in the new citation sentence is consistent with the parameter count c3 quantitative_verification established.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Unchanged. No monitor.py entry fetches or compares road-distance data for WIN-069; it is a static published claim.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Unchanged. Standard physics has no 'road-distance-vs-geodesic' predictive framework; road excess is treated as a topography/network-engineering question. V13 invents a topology-based explanation rather than relabeling a standard one.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Unchanged. Australian road distances, WGS84 inter-city distances, Nullarbor segment lengths, and AGD66->GDA94 datum history all predate V13; the dome adopted known quantities and labeled matching them as confirmation.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Current WIN-069 reads: 'V13 topology + refractive profile are dome-derived; numerical parameters are fit to globe data (AGD66->GDA94), but the geometric framework (ring/toroidal, V13) originates from dome model structure.' This is now consistent with the EXP-054 verdict boilerplate ('numerical value is one the dome framework derives from its own geometric or symbolic parameters') and matches WIN-057's tag for the same V13 topology. c3 hole #1 is resolved.

holes_found.description: CITATION JOURNAL NAME LIKELY INCORRECT. The new terrain-citation sentence cites 'Giacomin & Levinson 2015, Transportation Planning and Technology.' The paper that matches this authorship+year+topic is Giacomin, D.J. & Levinson, D.M., 'Road network circuity in metropolitan areas,' which was published in Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design, vol. 42, no. 6, pp. 1040-1053 (2015) — NOT in Transportation Planning and Technology. This is the exact class of error c1/c2 caught on WIN-008, WIN-009, and WIN-014 (wrong journal or wrong DOI for an otherwise real paper). A dome defender running any citation-verification pass will flag this trivially: the authors-and-year match resolves to Environment and Planning B, and no Giacomin+Levinson+2015+Transportation Planning and Technology entry exists. Because this was added to close an 'unsourced counter-model' hole, a wrong journal name partially reopens the hole — our citation now arguably fails the same scrutiny we apply to the dome's unsourced claims.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the Giacomin & Levinson 2015 venue against the paper's abstract page before the next live deploy. If 'Road network circuity in metropolitan areas' is the intended reference, change 'Transportation Planning and Technology' to 'Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design' (vol. 42, no. 6). If a different Giacomin & Levinson 2015 paper is intended (they have multiple co-authored works), add the paper title inline so readers can disambiguate. Separately, verify Huber & Rust 2016, R Journal for the same concern — a 'detour'/'circuity' R-package vignette may or may not have appeared in the R Journal and should be checked against the R Journal author index.

holes_found.description: STATISTIC MISMATCH BETWEEN COMPARED QUANTITIES. The new sentence reads: 'producing sub-1% mean-ratio prediction errors against observed road distances with a single free parameter, versus V13 >=4 free parameters for 6.2%.' V13's 6.2% figure is a cross-equatorial RMSE (per-route residuals squared-averaged). The 'sub-1% mean-ratio prediction error' for circuity models is a mean-ratio fit across large samples — a different statistical object. A capable dome defender will say: 'You are comparing our per-route RMSE to a terrain-model mean-ratio fit. Those are not the same statistic. Show us a terrain model with published per-route RMSE on our 12-route sample, or concede you do not have a fair RMSE-vs-RMSE comparison.' The EXP-231 document's own anticipated_objections section acknowledges this narrower reading but the prose as written does not cover the gap. This is a narrow version of the same 'unsourced counter-model' hole the patch set out to close.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) rephrase to a same-statistic comparison. Example: 'producing sub-1% prediction error on mean road-to-geodesic ratio across continental samples (V13 same-hemisphere mean error is 10.2% on the same statistic)' — this trades the RMSE framing for a mean-ratio comparison in which V13 also loses, and the 10.2% same-hemisphere number is already on the page. Or (b) add one clarifying parenthetical: 'using mean-ratio fit as the comparison statistic; a direct per-route RMSE comparison would require running a circuity model on the 12-route Australian sample, which has not been published.' Either preserves the added value of the citation while closing the defender's exit.

holes_found.description: 0.4% ANCHOR IS A LOOSE UPPER BOUND. The new sentence states the 90-Mile Straight 'deviates from its great-circle geodesic by less than 0.4%.' As c3 quantitative_verification established, a physical straight 146.6 km road on the WGS84 ellipsoid has a geodesic deviation well under 0.01% (sub-meter ellipsoid-chord separation). The 0.4% upper bound matches the TLDR figure and is therefore the right anchor for that TLDR, but it is ~40x looser than the actual geometric bound. A careful reader who computes the true deviation will notice we accepted a round-figure bound rather than reporting the much tighter fact. This does not undermine the argument (the argument goes the right way either way) but it leaves a cosmetic gap: the strongest possible wording would be 'less than 0.01%' (or 'effectively zero, well under 0.01%') rather than 'less than 0.4%.'
holes_found.recommendation: Low-priority improvement. If TLDRs are ever revised (see carryover hole below), replace 'only 0.4% deviation' with 'essentially zero deviation' and replace the detail_evidence 0.4% figure with '<0.01%'. Otherwise leave as-is; the TLDR/detail consistency achieved by EXP-231 is more important than tightening the bound.

holes_found.description: CARRYOVER: c3 HOLE #2 (10,000x vs mm-GNSS COMPARATOR MISLABELLING) REMAINS OPEN IN detail_evidence AND TLDR FIELDS. EXP-231 was deliberately scoped to ISS-1432/1433 only; hole #2 was deferred. Current state: detail_evidence still reads 'errors roughly four to five orders of magnitude larger than standard geodesy (10,000x vs. millimeter-level GNSS positioning, up to 250,000x vs. sub-mm VLBI geodesy)'; tldr_evidence still reads '10,000x worse than standard geodetic accuracy (sub-meter)'; tldr_verdict still reads '10,000x worse than standard geodesy.' Quantitative_verification: 6.2% / 2.5e-7 (1 m on 4,000 km = sub-meter) ~ 2.5e5 ~ 250,000x — NOT 10,000x. 6.2% / 2.5e-10 (mm on 4,000 km) ~ 2.5e8 — also NOT 10,000x. The TLDRs in particular are the first thing readers see, and they contain the wrong-by-25x figure. The finding was rewritten separately (to 'orders of magnitude worse than sub-meter geodesy'), which is the right direction — but the correction has not propagated to the TLDRs or detail_evidence. Expected per EXP-231 out_of_scope, but still live.
holes_found.recommendation: Queue a follow-up EXP that propagates the finding's 'orders of magnitude worse than sub-meter geodesy' framing into (a) detail_evidence 10,000x-to-250,000x range sentence, (b) tldr_evidence, and (c) tldr_verdict. Simplest same-statistic fix: replace the ratio throughout with 'roughly 250,000x (5 orders of magnitude) worse than sub-meter geodesy' — this matches the 6.2% / 2.5e-7 arithmetic and is consistent with the finding's rewrite. Reference c3 hole #2 recommendation for the alternative relabeling option.

holes_found.description: CARRYOVER: c3 HOLE #6 ('thousands of possible Australian road pairs') STILL OPEN. The selection-bias paragraph in detail_evidence still reads 'the dome cites 12 roads and 9 cross-links as its validation sample from thousands of possible Australian road pairs.' A defender can reasonably say: 'We did not sample from thousands of random pairs — we selected major transcontinental routes with published references.' Expected per EXP-231 out_of_scope; low priority but worth queueing a micro-patch.
holes_found.recommendation: Queue micro-patch: replace 'thousands of possible Australian road pairs' with 'the larger set of major transcontinental and inter-city Australian road pairs' or a specific defensible count (e.g., '~50 pairs among Australia's 15 largest cities'). This avoids giving the defender a number to haggle with.

stronger_arguments.description: The citation-integrity hole (moderate hole #1 above) is itself a meta-argument: our review is more careful than the dome's because we actually cite sources and verify them. Every incorrect citation we allow to stand erodes that edge. A single post-publication correction of 'Transportation Planning and Technology' -> 'Environment and Planning B' is cheap and measurably strengthens the 'our citations verify, theirs do not' framing that recurs across Part 3 and Part 10.
sources: Curmudgeon c1/c2 record on WIN-008, WIN-009, WIN-014 (wrong DOIs / journals caught and fixed)

stronger_arguments.description: The 'sub-1% mean-ratio prediction error' phrasing can be made more damaging by pivoting to the same-hemisphere 10.2% figure the finding now leads with. If circuity models routinely achieve sub-1% mean-ratio error and V13's same-hemisphere mean error is 10.2% (the exact quantity where V13 is strongest per its own narrative), then V13 is at least 10x worse than the simplest published non-dome alternative on the statistic V13 claims to predict best. This is a sharper and more defensible comparison than the cross-hemisphere 6.2% RMSE vs terrain-mean-ratio comparison we currently write.
sources: Dome site Tier-1 Steelman Evidence Ladder (10.2% same-hemisphere figure)
Giacomin & Levinson 2015 (verified journal TBD) — sub-1% mean-ratio fit across metropolitan networks

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-231 correctly identified and closed the two most citation-pickable holes from c3 (TLDR anchor gap, unsourced counter-model). The 90-Mile Straight anchor is a genuinely strong concrete reference — it is the named segment the dome's own pages discuss, and stating '<0.4%' gives the TLDR figure a paragraph-level home. The decision to cite two circuity papers (tooling anchor + empirical range) is minimal, not stuffed. The quantitative framing (detour index 1.05-1.20; >=4 V13 parameters vs 1 for circuity models) turns a name-drop into a comparison.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The verdict does not depend on these two patches either way — 'Misleading' was robust to both states. The new value of the patches is defensive: they remove two specific citable pickup-holes from the c3 advocate_mode. The remaining holes (wrong journal name, statistic-mismatch, and carryover c3 #2/#6) still give a careful defender material to attack, but less of it, and none of it flips the verdict. Moving from defense_survives=4 at c3 to defense_survives=2 at c4 is the right trajectory.

deeper_analysis: This review is the companion c4 to the c3 dense findings from 2026-04-22. c3 flagged six holes (two major, three moderate, one minor) plus the tag/text contradiction from EXP-054 boilerplate. Between c3 and this c4 review, three of those six holes have been closed: #1 (tag/text contradiction) by a separate derives_from_dome flip + evidence-string rewrite, #3 (TLDR anchor gap) by EXP-231 patch 1, and #4 (unsourced counter-model) by EXP-231 patch 2. Hole #5 is partially closed (finding rewritten away from '10,000x'; detail_evidence and both TLDRs still contain the miscalibrated 10,000x figure). Holes #2 and #6 are explicitly out-of-scope for EXP-231 and remain live. The two moderate NEW holes this review surfaces (wrong Giacomin & Levinson journal name; mean-ratio vs RMSE statistic mismatch) are both side-effects of the added citation: a citation-based defense tightens the argument but also opens two new citation-style vulnerabilities the dome defender can now exploit. The net trajectory is positive — c3 advocate_mode defense_survives=4 downgrades to c4 defense_survives=2 — but the text is not yet at the 'bulletproof' state the curmudgeon standard requires. Recommended path: (a) verify and correct the Giacomin & Levinson venue (simplest single-word fix), (b) add one parenthetical defusing the RMSE-vs-mean-ratio mismatch, (c) queue the long-standing '10,000x' propagation EXP separately. None of this is blocking; the verdict ('Misleading') holds with higher confidence than c3 (0.90 vs 0.88) because the two patches close the strongest two hole categories and the remaining holes are quantitative-framing issues that, once corrected, do not disturb the verdict.

citations_verified: 90-Mile Straight 146.6 km, Balladonia to Caiguna — matches published length of the Nullarbor straight segment on the Eyre Highway.

<0.4% road-to-geodesic deviation on a 146.6 km physical straight road on WGS84 — consistent with the <0.01% geometric upper bound established in c3 quantitative_verification; 0.4% is a loose but correct upper bound.
citations_failed: Giacomin & Levinson 2015, 'Transportation Planning and Technology' — LIKELY WRONG VENUE. The paper 'Road network circuity in metropolitan areas' by D.J. Giacomin and D.M. Levinson was published in Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design (vol. 42, no. 6, pp. 1040-1053, 2015). Without access to an external citation search in this run this cannot be 100% confirmed, but the title/author/year/topic signature strongly matches the Environment and Planning B paper, not a Transportation Planning and Technology paper. Flagged for verification before next publish.
citations_unchecked: Huber & Rust 2016, R Journal — author+year+venue claim not verified in this run; a 'detour' or 'circuity' R-package vignette-style R Journal paper is plausible but should be checked against the R Journal author index before next publish.
Internal anchor #posthoc-standard (Part 3.1b) — depends on built HTML.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: c3 WIN-057 <-> WIN-069 derives_from_dome inconsistency is now resolved: WIN-069 is tagged true with an evidence string that explicitly names the calibration-vs-structure split. No new cross-WIN inconsistency introduced by the EXP-231 prose additions. WIN-027 (relabels_standard=true, derives_from_dome=false) remains a distinct combination for the quadratic-distance-excess relabel, which does not reference V13 topology directly.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A c4 dome defender steelman: Your review now cites Huber & Rust 2016 and Giacomin & Levinson 2015 to support 'terrain-based models outperform V13.' Good — except (1) the Giacomin & Levinson citation lists the wrong journal. That is the exact kind of citation integrity failure you catch the dome on. (2) Your sub-1% comparison silently swaps statistics: V13 is measured by RMSE, your circuity citation by mean-ratio fit. Apples vs oranges. (3) Your own TLDR says '10,000x worse than standard geodetic accuracy (sub-meter)' — but sub-meter on 4,000 km gives a ratio ~250,000x, not 10,000x. You asked readers to trust your numerical framing; your TLDR is off by a factor of 25. The case for V13 being worse than standard practice is still probably correct; the case for your review being internally rigorous is weakening.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Two of the three sub-arguments in this defense are trivially fixed: the wrong journal name is a one-word edit, and the RMSE-vs-mean-ratio gap is a one-parenthetical clarification. The third (10,000x in TLDRs and detail_evidence) is the long-standing c3 hole #2 that needs its own EXP to propagate the finding rewrite into the TLDRs and detail_evidence. After all three are patched, defense_survives drops to 1 — the defender still has the finding's general 'standard geodesy is millimeter-precision and yours is 6.2%' narrative framing to attack rhetorically, but no numerical or citation-integrity material to pick from.

claims_checked: Huber & Rust 2016 and Giacomin & Levinson 2015 are real-sounding references with verifiable authorship
circuity models produce 'sub-1% mean-ratio prediction errors with a single free parameter'
Carryover: '10,000x worse than mm-GNSS' in TLDRs and detail_evidence still off vs actual 6.2%/2.5e-10 ~ 10^8
quantitative_verification.computations: 90-Mile Straight: named segment Balladonia (S 32.3 deg, E 123.6 deg) to Caiguna (S 32.3 deg, E 125.5 deg); published length 146.6 km — matches. Geodesic across ~1.9 deg longitude at S 32 deg on WGS84 ellipsoid: local straight-line (chord-on-surface) deviation from geodesic < ~1 m over ~150 km, giving a geodesic deviation ratio of < 1e-5, i.e. <0.001%. Stated '<0.4%' is therefore a correct but conservative upper bound (~400x looser than the geometric bound). V13 parameters: H_0 (~8,537 km), lambda (decay, ~8,619 km), equatorial ring radius (14,105 km), and angular scaling factor (~0.9941) — 4 free parameters is correct; '>=4' is defensible and conservative. Circuity 'sub-1% mean-ratio prediction error': supported by the reported detour-index range 1.05-1.20 (mean-ratio variance ~5% around a one-parameter fit implies sub-1% fit error on the mean). Carryover '10,000x vs mm-GNSS' numerics: 6.2% / 2.5e-10 = 2.48e8 ~ 2.5e8 — the correct ratio vs mm-precision is ~10^8, not 10^4, so the TLDRs '10,000x worse than sub-meter' over-states precision of the comparator by 25x (sub-meter would yield 250,000x).
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: Giacomin & Levinson 2015 venue ('Transportation Planning and Technology') almost certainly should read 'Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design' — see holes_found. The 10,000x carryover is a c3 hole #2 remnant in detail_evidence and both TLDRs; not a new issue, but still live.
